data.title: China's BYD looks to assemble EVs in Pakistan from 2026
data.description:
• Chinese EV giant BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) (OTCPK:BYDDY) is looking to roll out its first car assembled in Pakistan by July or August 2026 to capture growing demand in the region for electric and plug-in hybrid vehicles, a company executive told Reuters.
  • BYD, the world's top EV maker, has been expanding rapidly outside its home market, where it is in a strong price war.
  • The plant has been under construction since April near Karachi in a partnership between BYD and Mega Motor Company, a subsidiary of Pakistani utility Hub Power, the report said.
  • It would initially have the capacity to produce 25,000 units a year on a double shift.
  • The EV maker also plans to start manufacturing cars earlier than expected at a new plant in Turkey, where labor costs are lower, as per earlier reports.

data.title: Volkswagen slashes full-year outlook, Q2 profit plunges amid hefty US tariffs
data.description:
• Volkswagen press release (OTCPK:VWAGY) has lowered its full-year guidance and reported a sharp drop in second-quarter profit, as the auto giant navigates the disruptive impact of U.S. tariffs.
  • For the second quarter, the company recorded 2.26 million vehicle sales. Production, however, totaled 2.33 million units, a 0.6% decrease from the previous year.
  • The operating profit of 3.83 billion euros ($4.49 billion) for the three months through June, down 29% from 5.4 billion euros a year ago.
  • Revenue of €80.81B (-3.0% Y/Y).
  • The automotive giant is navigating a challenging global trade environment, with the tariffs, notably a 27.5% levy on certain vehicles, significantly impacting its North American operations and overall profitability.
  • 4.36 million vehicle sales in H1 2025, slightly above H1 2024 (4.34 million vehicles).
  • Outlook for the year 2025 as of July 25, 2025: 
  • The Volkswagen Group expects sales revenue to be in line with the previous year’s figure (previously: increase of up to 5 percent). The Group’s operating return on sales is expected to range between 4.0 and 5.0 percent (previously: 5.5 to 6.5 percent).
  • In the Automotive Division, the Volkswagen Group continues to expect an investment ratio between 12 and 13 percent in 2025. Automotive net cash flow for 2025 is expected to be between EUR 1 and EUR 3 billion (previously: EUR 2 to EUR 5 billion). This includes cash outflows for investments for the future as well as for restructuring measures. Net liquidity in the Automotive Division in 2025 is expected to be between EUR 31 and EUR 33 billion (previously: EUR 34 to EUR 37 billion).
  • The Group continues to pursue its objective of maintaining a solid financing and liquidity policy. At the lower end of the forecast ranges for operating result, net cash flow and net liquidity, it is assumed that in particular the current US import tariffs of 27.5 percent will continue to apply in the second half of 2025; at the upper end, it is assumed that these tariffs will be reduced to 10 percent.

data.title: Asia stocks fall as markets brace for Trump tariff deadline, central bank meetings
data.description:
Asian shares pulled back on Friday, with Japanese markets notably dipping from a recent record, as investors chose to secure profits. This comes ahead of a pivotal week featuring a deadline for U.S. President Donald Trump's tariffs and multiple central bank meetings.

Gold prices fell to around $3,360 per ounce on Friday, marking a third consecutive session of losses, as concerns over tariffs continued to ease.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 0.87% to below 41,700, while the Topix Index dropped 0.6% to 2,960 on Friday, as investors booked profits following a sharp rally that pushed both indexes to new highs. The Japanese yen slipped to around 147.5 per dollar on Friday, marking its second consecutive day of losses, as markets digested the implications of the new US-Japan trade agreement.

Japan’s index of coincident economic indicators—which tracks key data such as factory output, employment, and retail sales—stood at 116.0 in May 2025, slightly above the flash estimate of 115.9 but unchanged from April's reading.

Japan’s leading economic index—which reflects the outlook for the coming months based on indicators such as job offers and consumer sentiment—was revised down to 104.8 in May 2025 from a preliminary estimate of 105.3. 

On the data front, Tokyo’s core inflation in July came in below expectations but remained well above the Bank of Japan’s 2% target, reinforcing market expectations for another rate hike this year. 

China (SHCOMP) fell 0.23% to below 3,600, while the Shenzhen Component edged down 0.1% to 11,180 on Friday, as investors turned cautious ahead of further US-China trade negotiations next week, and the offshore yuan weakened to around 7.15 against the US dollar on Friday, retreating from an over eight-month high reached in the previous session.

China on Thursday released a draft amendment to its pricing law to address excessive competition and price wars, amid ongoing deflationary pressures. The proposed changes aim to curb practices like selling below cost to eliminate competitors or monopolize markets, except in cases of legitimate discounts such as for seasonal or overstocked goods. 

Investor attention is also focused on China’s industrial profits data due this weekend.

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.82% to 25,410 on Friday morning, down for the first time in six sessions, as traders took profits after markets hit a near 4-year top earlier in the week.

India (SENSEX) fell 0.75% to 81,827 on Friday, extending losses from the previous session and hitting its lowest level since mid-June.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.50% dropped 0.5% to 8,664 in morning trade on Friday, extending losses from the previous session, with nearly all sectors in the red. The Australian dollar fell to around $0.658 on Friday, extending losses from the previous session, as investors remained cautious ahead of key inflation data due next week. 

Meanwhile, investors are closely watching developments in US–Australia trade relations. In the latest development, Australia has agreed to ease restrictions on US beef imports—a decision that follows tensions over previous trade limitations.

In the U.S., on Thursday, all three major indexes ended mixed, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 closed at fresh record highs on Thursday, rising 0.1% and 0.2% respectively, supported by strong earnings from Alphabet that bolstered investor confidence in AI investments. 

U.S. stock futures edged higher on Friday after the major averages ended mixed in the previous session: Dow +0.24%; S&P 500 +0.23%; Nasdaq +0.18%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia:

Trump says U.S. strikes ‘massive’ trade deal with Japan, imposes 15% tariffs

Japan vows more rice imports from U.S. as part of trade deal

Japanese PM Shigeru Ishiba to step down

RBA holds rates steady, citing unclear inflation picture in July minutes

Japan's core inflation eases to 3.3% in June, slowest pace since March

data.title: Trump says he wants Musk and his companies to 'thrive' in U.S.
data.description:
President Donald Trump denied any intentions of destroying Elon Musk’s businesses by revoking federal subsidies and added that he wants all the U.S. companies to survive as their good performance is better for the country.

"Everyone is stating that I will destroy Elon's companies by taking away some, if not all, of the large scale subsidies he receives from the U.S. Government," Trump said in a social media post.
 
“This is not so! I want Elon, and all businesses within our Country, to THRIVE, in fact, THRIVE like never before!”
 
“The better they do, the better the USA does, and that’s good for all of us,” Trump wrote.
 
The tech billionaire responded with a simple “Phew,” posted on X, expressing relief at Trump’s assertion that he would not actively target Musk’s companies.
 
Earlier in the summer, there were reports of Trump considering revoking some of Musk’s government contracts and ending electric vehicle incentives, which raised investor concerns at Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA).
 
The comments come after a public feud between the former close allies after Musk said that Trump's "big, beautiful" tax bill would bankrupt America.
 
Musk has previously questioned the narrative around government support, arguing that many of the “subsidies” simply do not exist. His aerospace and defense contractor SpaceX also has massive contract exposure in the U.S.
 
On Wednesday, Musk said on the Q2 earnings call that Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) could experience a “few rough quarters” due to tariff costs and the expiration of federal EV benefits by the end of September.

ETFs to track the broader U.S. market: (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Enova expects Q3 2025 adjusted EPS growth of 20%–25% amid leadership transition and strong SMB performance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Enova International (ENVA) Q2 2025

Management View

  • David Fisher, President, CEO & Chairman, announced a significant leadership transition: "After over 12 years serving as the Chairman and CEO of Enova, I've decided that now is the time to transition into the role of Executive Chairman effective January 1, 2026." Steve Cunningham, current CFO, will step into the CEO role while Scott Cornelis, Treasurer, will become CFO. Fisher emphasized, "This move was thoroughly, thoughtfully and deliberately planned as part of the company's disciplined and measured long-term leadership transition planning."
  • Fisher stated, "For the fifth quarter in a row, we generated greater than 20% year-over-year growth in revenue, originations, and adjusted EPS." He highlighted robust demand and profitability, particularly in small business products which now represent 65% of the total portfolio.
  • Fisher noted, "SMB revenue increased 30% year-over-year and 7% sequentially to a record $326 million, and our consumer revenue increased $428 million, 17% higher than a year ago and basically flat sequentially off an unexpectedly strong Q1."
  • Fisher said, "We do not anticipate any significant impact to our consumer business in the upcoming quarters" after quickly tightening credit models in response to slightly elevated defaults in one consumer product.
  • Fisher described the SMB business as benefiting from "our leading brand presence, scale and low levels of competition," with origination growth and stability in credit performance.
  • Steven Cunningham, CFO, reported, "Total company revenue of $764 million increased 22% from the second quarter of 2024, exceeding our expectations and driven by 20% year-over-year growth in total company combined loan and finance receivables balances on an amortized basis."
  • Cunningham added, "For the third quarter of 2025, we expect total company revenue to be more than 15% higher than the third quarter of 2024."
  • Cunningham stated, "We continue to deliver solid profitability and strong returns on equity this quarter. Compared to the second quarter of 2024, adjusted EPS, a non-GAAP measure, increased 46% to $3.23 per diluted share, delivering an annualized second quarter return on equity of 28%."

Outlook

  • Cunningham stated, "For the third quarter, we expect consolidated revenue to be more than 15% higher than the third quarter of 2024, with a net revenue margin in the range of 55% to 60%."
  • Cunningham also projected, "These expectations should lead to adjusted EPS for the third quarter of 2025 that is 20% to 25% higher than the third quarter of 2024. For the full year, we now expect revenue growth compared to the full year of 2024 of around 20% and adjusted EPS growth of around 30%."
  • Management emphasized that expectations depend on the macroeconomic environment and the resulting impact on demand, payment rates, and originations mix.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q2 reached $764 million, a 22% increase year-over-year. Small business revenue grew 30% to $326 million, while consumer revenue increased 17% to $428 million.
  • Originations totaled $1.8 billion, up 28% year-over-year. Small business originations rose to $1.2 billion, a 35% increase.
  • Adjusted EPS for Q2 was $3.23 per diluted share, reflecting a 46% year-over-year increase.
  • The consolidated net charge-off ratio for the quarter declined to 8.1% from 8.6% last quarter. Consumer net charge-off ratio for Q2 was 14.5%, down 70 basis points sequentially.
  • Total operating expenses were 32% of revenue, with marketing expenses at 19% of revenue. Operations and technology expenses were 8% of revenue, and G&A expenses were 5% of revenue.
  • The company ended Q2 with $1.1 billion in liquidity, including $388 million of cash and marketable securities.

Q&A

  • Moshe Orenbuch, TD Cowen: Asked about confidence in the consumer portfolio and the impact on originations in the second half. Fisher responded that only one of five consumer products had elevated defaults, which was quickly addressed by tightening credit models, stating, "Credit came back in line, just like we would expect."
  • Orenbuch also inquired about SMB originations. Fisher explained, "Small business just had a couple of rock-solid quarters in a row... Credit has been incredibly stable."
  • David Scharf, Citizens: Asked if the product with higher delinquencies was the highest APR product. Fisher clarified product segmentation and noted fluctuations are normal and managed.
  • Scharf asked Fisher about the timing of the leadership change. Fisher said, "The business is on super stable footing. And if it wasn't, I wouldn't be making this change now."
  • William Ryan, Seaport: Asked about marketing efficiency. Cunningham responded, "It was largely driven by a little bit lower origination expectation or a little bit lower originations in consumer than we expected... offset by the strength in small business."
  • Ryan questioned the consumer portfolio yield decline. Cunningham said, "That was really more of -- you'll see that ratio can move around a little bit as we're opportunistic across those products... probably hang around the 115 to 120 as you look out the rest of the year."
  • John Hecht, Jefferies: Asked about marketing channel changes. Fisher replied, "At a high level, we're in the exact same marketing channels we were in 10 years ago. But... it's incredibly different."
  • Hecht asked about funding and balance sheet management. Cunningham explained, "The credit markets have been pretty favorable... we're pretty well positioned on the balance sheet."
  • Vincent Caintic, BTIG: Asked about macro trends and growth. Fisher said, "We think even with the solid macro trends, it's a good time to be balanced between originations growth and risk."
  • Kyle Joseph, Stephens: Asked about competition. Fisher stated, "The competitive dynamics on the small business side are more stable. There's fewer players."
  • John Rowan, Janney: Asked about the impact of heightened DQs on fair value marks. Cunningham said, "The fair value marks have been very stable."
  • Rowan inquired about debt sensitivity. Cunningham replied, "It's been about 50% floating for a while, and it's really most sensitive to SOFR."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive and congratulatory tone regarding leadership transitions, with a focus on operational details and risk management, and raised clarifying questions about credit quality and marketing efficiency.
  • Management consistently projected confidence, emphasizing stability and adaptability, with Fisher stating, "If it wasn't, I wouldn't be making this change now," and Cunningham highlighting stable performance and strong positioning.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts sustained a positive sentiment, with little evidence of skepticism or defensiveness in tone.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to more specific EPS and revenue growth targets for Q3 and full year 2025 compared to prior quarter’s more general outlook.
  • Strategy remains focused on balanced growth and risk, with increased emphasis on leadership transition as a key development.
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter centered more on operational execution and leadership stability, versus last quarter’s focus on credit performance and competitive dynamics.
  • Key metrics, including revenue, originations, and adjusted EPS, all displayed sequential and year-over-year growth, with stable credit quality highlighted again.
  • Management tone on both calls was confident, but the current quarter included more forward-looking commentary tied to leadership and risk management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Fisher acknowledged "slightly elevated default metrics from new customers" in one consumer product early in the quarter, attributing this to "uncertainty around the impacts of tariffs on the job market and inflation." The company responded by "quickly tightened our credit models to slow originations."
  • Management stated, "Our unit economics framework, combined with our sophisticated technology and analytics, are designed to assess risk in real time."
  • Concerns about macroeconomic volatility, including tariffs and inflation, were noted, with management emphasizing their ability to adapt rapidly and diversify between consumer and SMB segments.
  • Analysts raised questions regarding the sustainability of credit quality and potential risks from macro trends, with management reiterating the normalcy of short-term fluctuations and their mitigation strategies.

Final Takeaway

Enova International delivered another strong quarter, highlighted by a planned leadership transition and robust growth across both small business and consumer lending. The company reported record originations, solid revenue growth, stable credit performance, and reaffirmed its commitment to a balanced and adaptable strategy. Management provided clear forward guidance, projecting double-digit revenue and EPS growth for the remainder of 2025, while maintaining a focus on operational efficiency and proactive risk management amid evolving macroeconomic conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: FinWise Bancorp outlines path to $50M–$100M credit enhanced balances by year-end as originations rise 17%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FinWise Bancorp (FINW) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Kent Landvatter stated the company “delivered strong loan originations, maintained solid revenue and remain[ed] disciplined on expense management, all of which contributed to growing profitability.” He highlighted that tangible book per share increased to $13.51 from $13.42 in the prior quarter and announced the company's addition to the U.S. Small Cap Russell 2000 Index. Landvatter expressed optimism about the long-term growth supported by infrastructure investments and new product launches, noting, “we are proud that our publicly traded stock was recently added to the U.S. Small Cap Russell 2000 Index.”
  • Landvatter discussed measured growth expectations: “We continue to look for growth to progress gradually through 2025 and throughout 2026, driven mainly by our credit enhanced product, originations from existing SP programs and incremental growth from programs that were signed late in 2024 and early in 2025.”
  • The CEO confirmed ongoing evaluation of higher-yielding loan balances, stating exposure will remain under 10% of the portfolio, and outlined strategic ambitions for the BIN and payment products to shift deposit mix away from higher-cost CDs. He projected that “2027 could begin to reflect the growth benefits of our broader banking and payments offering,” with potential for return on average equity to rebound into the low to mid-teens range and return on average assets exceeding 2%.
  • Bank CEO James Noone reported origination volume of $1.5 billion in Q2, marking a 17% increase quarter-over-quarter and a 27% increase year-over-year. Noone attributed this growth to strategic programs announced late in 2024 and a seasonal rebound from higher-yielding partners. He noted that credit enhanced balances reached $12 million at quarter end, with expectations for these assets to reach $50 million to $100 million by year-end.
  • Noone added that “quarterly SBA 7(a) loan originations increased 24% quarter-over-quarter and are up over 140% from the same quarter last year.” He reported aggregate SBA guaranteed balances and strategic program loans held for sale made up 43% of the total portfolio at the end of Q2.
  • CFO Robert Wahlman stated, “For the second quarter, we reported net income of $4.1 million or $0.29 per diluted common share.” He cited strong originations, a rise in net interest income, solid fee income, and contained expense growth as key drivers. Wahlman also noted, “average loan balances, including held for sale and held for investment loans totaled $634 million for the quarter compared to $565 million in the prior quarter.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated guidance for credit enhanced assets to reach $50 million to $100 million by the end of Q4 2025. Landvatter projected gradual growth through 2025 and 2026, driven by existing and new programs. He highlighted that the material impact of BIN and payment products is expected in late 2026, with broader profitability benefits anticipated in 2027. The company continues to target a return on average equity rebounding into the low to mid-teens and return on average assets above 2% as the strategy matures.

Financial Results

  • The company reported net income of $4.1 million or $0.29 per diluted common share. Average loan balances increased to $634 million from $565 million in the prior quarter. Net interest income was $14.7 million compared to $14.3 million in Q1. Net interest margin was 7.81%, down from 8.27%, mainly due to accrued interest reversals and the addition of higher quality but lower-yielding loans. Fee income rose to $10.3 million from $7.8 million in the prior quarter, driven by credit enhancement income, strategic program fees, and gain on sale of loans. Noninterest expense was $14.9 million versus $14.3 million, primarily due to increases in salaries and benefits from annual reviews and incentive programs. The reported efficiency ratio improved to 59.5% from 64.8% in the prior quarter. The effective tax rate fell to 24.5% from 28.1%.

Q&A

  • Brett Rabatin, Hovde Group: Asked about the relationship between credit enhanced fee income and balances. CEO Landvatter explained, “it is the dollar-for-dollar offset for the provision for credit losses related to the credit enhanced portfolio…as the balances increase, you will see, again, this large -- this fee income.”
  • Rabatin also queried about funding costs amid loan growth. CFO Wahlman responded, “The short-term answer is that we are dependent upon wholesale funding, principally certificates of deposits…Longer term, we are working diligently to bring some of the payments business into our -- into the bank.”
  • Rabatin inquired about net charge-offs. CFO Wahlman clarified, “The NCOs moved up in the quarter from 2.2% to 2.8%. And the quarter-over-quarter difference is really just some SBA charge-offs...But there's no like trend there.”
  • Joseph Yanchunis, Raymond James: Asked about AI and stablecoins. Landvatter said, “stablecoins for us specifically are not a near-term initiative, though we're looking into various potential opportunities.” On AI, he noted, “most of our AI would be back office focused” for fraud detection and operational efficiency.
  • Yanchunis also probed on credit enhanced loan balances and provisioning. Noone detailed that most incremental balances came on late in Q2 and that provisioning is program-specific.
  • Yanchunis requested an update on partner health and pipeline. Noone stated, “partners are healthy, loan demand is healthy. And as a result, originations are up.” Landvatter added, “The pipeline is really good…our target would be for another 1 to 2 lending partners before year-end.”
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens: Sought clarity on credit enhanced growth pacing. Noone confirmed the $50 million to $100 million year-end target and outlined that four programs are live, with a fifth expected by year-end. Terrell also asked about average held-for-sale balances; Landvatter said current levels are a fair representation going forward.
  • Terrell questioned financial targets for 2027. Landvatter and Wahlman described the targets as conservative and sustainable, contingent on capital and growth rates.
  • Terrell asked about future expenses. Wahlman indicated no notable investments expected and that headcount increases will be linked to revenue growth, affirming the expectation for positive operating leverage.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for clarity on the sustainability of credit enhanced fee income, funding strategies amid continued originations, and future operating efficiency, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone, with several questions on scalability and margin sustainability.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive outlook, using phrases such as “we are well positioned,” “we believe they offer meaningful earnings leverage over time,” and “we are excited about the platform we are building.” During Q&A, responses were thorough and addressed analysts’ concerns directly, indicating steady confidence.
  • Compared to Q1, both analysts and management displayed increased optimism, with less focus on macroeconomic risks and more on growth execution and product ramp.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Tangible book per share grew from $13.42 to $13.51. Origination volumes increased from $1.3 billion to $1.5 billion. Net income rose from $3.2 million to $4.1 million. Fee income and noninterest expense both increased, while the efficiency ratio improved. Net interest margin declined further, consistent with ongoing portfolio risk reduction. Management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q1 to greater confidence in Q2, especially regarding partner pipeline strength and the outlook for credit enhanced products. Analysts’ questions were more focused on growth sustainability than risk management this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that “multiple variables can impact this outlook, including target capital levels and the pace of our balance sheet growth.” Noone highlighted expectations for further migration of loans to NPA, stating that “for Q3, there could be up to $12 million of loans that could migrate to NPA.” The company continues to monitor variable rate SBA loans originated during lower rate environments. CFO Wahlman noted that further growth in lower-yielding assets could put gradual pressure on net interest margin.

Final Takeaway

FinWise Bancorp’s second quarter demonstrated significant progress in loan originations, fee income, and tangible book value, supported by healthy partner relationships and a robust strategic pipeline. The company reiterated its guidance for credit enhanced assets to reach $50 million to $100 million by year-end, and continues to expect gradual growth through 2025 and 2026, with meaningful profitability impact from new payments and BIN products anticipated in late 2026 and beyond. Management’s confidence was underpinned by continued execution on strategic programs, disciplined expense management, and a strong outlook for positive operating leverage as new products mature.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Liberty Energy Non-GAAP EPS of $0.12, revenue of $1B
data.description:
• Liberty Energy press release (NYSE:LBRT): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.12.

  • Distributed $13 million to shareholders through cash dividends.

data.title: Compañía de Minas Buenaventura GAAP EPS of $0.36 misses by $0.02, revenue of $369.5M beats by $43.86M
data.description:
• Compañía de Minas Buenaventura press release (NYSE:BVN): Q2 GAAP EPS of $0.36 misses by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $369.5M (+33.3% Y/Y) beats by $43.86M.

  • Buenaventura's cash position reached US$ 588.5 million by quarter’s end, June 30, 2025, while net debt amounted to US$ 271.7 million, resulting in a leverage ratio of 0.56x.

data.title: Newmont outlines $3B additional share buyback as divestments boost cash flow and capital returns
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Newmont Corporation (NEM) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Tom Palmer opened the call by addressing two fall of ground incidents at the Red Chris operation in British Columbia, which resulted in three workers being safely located in a refuge chamber. Palmer stated, "operations at Red Chris have been suspended whilst we're responding to the incident...Our focus is on restoring communications to the refuge chamber, safely reestablishing access underground and bringing our 3 teammates back to the surface and to their families and friends."
  • Palmer emphasized Newmont's continued focus on three key priorities for 2025: strengthening safety culture, stabilizing 11 managed operations, and executing on capital returns. He reported, "we produced 1.5 million ounces of gold and 36,000 tonnes of copper, remaining in line with our full year guidance and the indications we provided on our last call."
  • The company completed its noncore asset divestment program and expects to receive approximately $470 million in cash proceeds from the sale of shares in Greatland Gold and Discovery Silver. Palmer announced, "we now expect to generate $3 billion in after-tax cash proceeds this year from our divestment program."
  • A new $3 billion share repurchase program was approved, doubling the total authorization to $6 billion, "of which $2.8 billion has been executed to date."
  • President and COO Natascha Viljoen highlighted stronger-than-expected production at Cadia and Peñasquito, and noted cost savings at Lihir after operational improvements. She stated, "our second quarter operational results outperformed our previous expectations, effectively bookending the first half of the year and establishing a solid foundation for consistent delivery in the second half."
  • Palmer acknowledged the recent departure of CFO Karyn Ovelmen and confirmed Peter Wexler as Interim CFO, with no changes to financial policies or capital allocation strategy.
  • Palmer reported, "gold all-in sustaining costs for the quarter were slightly below our guidance for the full year at $1,593 an ounce on a co-product basis" and "on a by-product basis, our gold all-in sustaining costs for the second quarter were $1,375 an ounce."
  • The company generated $3 billion in adjusted EBITDA and reported an adjusted net income of $1.43 per share, with a record quarterly free cash flow of $1.7 billion.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated that Newmont is "firmly on track to meet our 2025 guidance, whilst also generating industry-leading free cash flow and consistently returning capital to shareholders through a predictable dividend and ongoing share repurchases."
  • Viljoen stated, "we remain firmly on track to meet the full year guidance ranges we issued in February."
  • Production at Cadia and Peñasquito is expected to decrease in the second half as mining transitions to lower-grade areas, and cost applicable to sales and all-in sustaining costs are anticipated to increase in the second half due to planned higher sustaining capital spend.
  • At Ahafo North, first gold pour is anticipated in the coming months, with commercial production expected in the fourth quarter.

Financial Results

  • The company reported $2.4 billion of cash flow from operations after working capital and $1.7 billion in free cash flow for the quarter.
  • Shareholder returns included the retirement of $372 million of debt and over $1 billion returned through dividends and share repurchases since the previous call.
  • Newmont finished the quarter with $6.2 billion in cash and an outstanding principal debt balance of $7.4 billion as of June 30.
  • The Board declared a fixed common second quarter dividend of $0.25 per share.
  • $750 million of shares were repurchased during the quarter, bringing the 2025 total to $1.5 billion.

Q&A

  • Lawson Winder, Bank of America: Asked about capital allocation priorities and copper's role. Palmer responded, "Our focus is internal. And the best use of our capital is to buy back Newmont stock...copper exposure will come from our organic growth."
  • Daniel Major, UBS: Inquired about management succession and impact of CFO departure. Palmer responded, "it's unfortunate, Karyn resigned, but...we have a very capable interim Chief Financial Officer. So absolutely no concerns in terms of the strength and capability of our business."
  • Fahad Tariq, Jefferies: Sought details on expected production declines at Cadia and Peñasquito. Viljoen explained, "we will see lower grades in -- of gold in the third quarter, and we will see higher grades of silver, lead and zinc."
  • Matthew Murphy, BMO: Asked about Lihir improvements and capital spend impact on 2026. Viljoen detailed ongoing productivity enhancements and asset management focus.
  • Joshua Wolfson, RBC: Questioned cost trends and inflation. Palmer noted, "the cost we expected to see, we're seeing this year...and labor is pretty much holding as we'd expect."
  • Daniel Morgan, Barrenjoey: Queried production guidance and why guidance was not lifted. Palmer stated, "we're firmly on track to meet our guidance."
  • Anita Soni, CIBC: Asked about Red Chris incident and capital spending shifts. Palmer confirmed emergency protocols are in place and Viljoen explained the deliberate timing of capital spend for asset integrity.
  • Tanya Jakusconek, Scotiabank: Explored portfolio composition and productivity opportunities. Palmer categorized certain equity positions as noncore, and Viljoen highlighted productivity upside at Lihir and Cerro Negro.
  • Hugo Nicolaci, Goldman Sachs: Sought details on Nevada and Boddington operations. Palmer deferred specifics on Nevada to the operator, while Viljoen described a 10% productivity uplift at Boddington due to autonomous haul fleet deployment.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral-to-positive tone, focusing on operational performance, capital allocation, and safety, with several expressing concern over the Red Chris incident and seeking reassurance on succession and guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident and measured tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing stability, internal focus, and commitment to capital returns. Palmer used phrases like "we remain firmly on track" and "we are confident" throughout.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained steady and focused, but the Red Chris incident introduced a more serious and urgent note regarding safety.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured an unexpected safety incident at Red Chris, which was not present in the previous quarter.
  • Management succession changes occurred with the departure of CFO Karyn Ovelmen and the appointment of Peter Wexler as interim CFO.
  • Guidance language and priorities remained consistent, with continued emphasis on safety, operational stability, and capital returns.
  • Financial performance improved, with higher cash flow from operations and record free cash flow compared to Q1.
  • The scale of share repurchase authorization doubled, reflecting increased confidence in capital return strategy.
  • Analysts' focus shifted to incident response, succession planning, and the sustainability of recent operational outperformance.

Risks and Concerns

  • The fall of ground incidents at Red Chris and the ongoing rescue operation were identified as the most immediate challenge, with all operations at the site suspended.
  • Palmer committed to a "thorough and independent investigation" and regular updates on the situation.
  • Planned production declines at Cadia, Peñasquito, and Lihir in the second half were discussed and attributed to mine sequencing and lower grades.
  • Higher sustaining capital and reclamation costs, as well as tax and royalty payments, are expected to impact free cash flow in the second half.
  • No new material inflationary or supply chain risks were highlighted, and labor and materials costs were described as stable.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored a strong operational and financial performance in the second quarter, highlighted by record free cash flow and a substantial increase in share repurchase authorization. The company remains committed to its 2025 guidance, internal capital allocation, and operational stability, but the immediate focus is on resolving the Red Chris incident and ensuring the safety of all personnel. Strategic discipline, a robust pipeline, and ongoing productivity improvements position Newmont to continue generating value for shareholders, with clear priorities on safety, cost management, and capital returns.
